title: Western Christianity

infobox.key: [Principal symbol of Christianity](/wiki/Christian_cross "Principal symbol of Christianity")
infobox.value: | * [Jesus](/wiki/Jesus_in_Christianity "Jesus in Christianity") * [Christ](/wiki/Christ_(title) "Christ (title)")  * [Nativity](/wiki/Nativity_of_Jesus "Nativity of Jesus") * [Baptism](/wiki/Baptism_of_Jesus "Baptism of Jesus") * [Ministry](/wiki/Ministry_of_Jesus "Ministry of Jesus") * [Crucifixion](/wiki/Crucifixion_of_Jesus "Crucifixion of Jesus") * [Resurrection](/wiki/Resurrection_of_Jesus "Resurrection of Jesus") * [Ascension](/wiki/Ascension_of_Jesus "Ascension of Jesus")

is one of two subdivisions of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") ([Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") being the other). Western Christianity is composed of the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and [Western Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), together with their offshoots such as the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"), [Independent Catholicism](/wiki/Independent_Catholicism "Independent Catholicism") and [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/15/Petersdom_von_Engelsburg_gesehen.jpg/250px-Petersdom_von_Engelsburg_gesehen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Petersdom_von_Engelsburg_gesehen.jpg)

[St. Peter's Basilica](/wiki/St._Peter%27s_Basilica "St. Peter's Basilica") in [Vatican City](/wiki/Vatican_City "Vatican City"), the largest church building in the world today[[2]](#cite_note-UNESCO_Article-2)

**Western Christianity** is one of two subdivisions of [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") ([Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity") being the other). Western Christianity is composed of the [Latin Church](/wiki/Latin_Church "Latin Church") and [Western Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism"), together with their offshoots such as the [Old Catholic Church](/wiki/Old_Catholic_Church "Old Catholic Church"), [Independent Catholicism](/wiki/Independent_Catholicism "Independent Catholicism") and [Restorationism](/wiki/Restorationism "Restorationism").

The large majority of the world's 2.3 billion Christians[[3]](#cite_note-3) are Western Christians. One major component, the Latin Church, developed under the [bishop of Rome](/wiki/Bishop_of_Rome "Bishop of Rome"). Out of the Latin Church emerged a wide variety of independent [Protestant denominations](/wiki/Protestant_denominations "Protestant denominations"), including [Lutheranism](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") and [Anglicanism](/wiki/Anglicanism "Anglicanism"), starting from the [Protestant Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") in the 16th century, as did Independent Catholicism in the 19th century. Thus, the term "Western Christianity" does not describe a single [communion](/wiki/Communion_(Christian) "Communion (Christian)") or [religious denomination](/wiki/Religious_denomination "Religious denomination") but is applied to distinguish all these denominations collectively from Eastern Christianity.

The establishment of the distinct Latin Church, a [particular church](/wiki/Particular_church "Particular church") *[sui iuris](/wiki/Sui_iuris "Sui iuris")* of the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church"), coincided with the consolidation of the [Holy See](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See") in [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), which claimed [primacy](/wiki/Papal_primacy "Papal primacy") since Antiquity. The Latin Church is distinct from the [Eastern Catholic Churches](/wiki/Eastern_Catholic_Churches "Eastern Catholic Churches"), also in [full communion](/wiki/Full_communion "Full communion") with the [Pope](/wiki/Pope "Pope") in [Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), and from the [Eastern Orthodox Church](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") and [Oriental Orthodox Churches](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodox_Churches "Oriental Orthodox Churches"), which are not in communion with Rome. These other churches are part of [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"). The terms "Western" and "Eastern" in this regard originated with geographical divisions mirroring the cultural divide between the [Hellenistic East and Latin West](/wiki/Greek_East_and_Latin_West "Greek East and Latin West") and the political divide between the [Western](/wiki/Western_Roman_Empire "Western Roman Empire") and [Eastern Roman empires](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"). During the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), adherents of the Latin Church, irrespective of ethnicity, commonly referred to themselves as "Latins" to distinguish themselves from [Eastern Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Christians "Eastern Christians") ("Greeks").[[4]](#cite_note-Orbilat-4)

Western Christianity has [played a prominent role](/wiki/Role_of_Christianity_in_civilization "Role of Christianity in civilization") in the shaping of [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture").[[5]](#cite_note-PerryChase2012-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-Caltron_J.H_Hayas-7)[[8]](#cite_note-Orlandis-8) With the expansion of [European colonialism](/wiki/European_colonialism "European colonialism") from the [Early Modern era](/wiki/Early_Modern_era "Early Modern era"), the Latin Church, in time along with its Protestant secessions, spread throughout the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas"), much of the [Philippines](/wiki/Philippines "Philippines"), [Southern Africa](/wiki/Southern_Africa "Southern Africa"), pockets of [West Africa](/wiki/West_Africa "West Africa"), and throughout [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia") and [New Zealand](/wiki/New_Zealand "New Zealand"). Thus, when used for historical periods after the 16th century, the term "Western Christianity" does not refer to a particular geographical area but is used as a collective term for all these.

Today, the geographical distinction between Western and Eastern Christianity is not nearly as absolute as in Antiquity or the Middle Ages, due to the spread of [Christian missionaries](/wiki/Christian_missionaries "Christian missionaries"), [migrations](/wiki/Human_migration "Human migration"), and [globalisation](/wiki/Globalisation "Globalisation"). As such, the adjectives "Western Christianity" and "Eastern Christianity" are typically used to refer to historical origins and differences in [theology](/wiki/Christian_theology "Christian theology") and [liturgy](/wiki/Christian_liturgy "Christian liturgy") rather than present geographical locations.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

While the Latin Church maintains the use of the [Latin liturgical rites](/wiki/Latin_liturgical_rites "Latin liturgical rites"), Protestant denominations and Independent Catholicism use various liturgical practices.

The earliest concept of Europe as a cultural sphere (instead of simply a geographic term) appeared during the [Carolingian Renaissance](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance") of the 9th century, which included territories that practiced Western Christianity at the time.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Western Christianity](/wiki/History_of_Western_Christianity "History of Western Christianity")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Gustav_Vasa_Bible_1541.jpg/250px-Gustav_Vasa_Bible_1541.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gustav_Vasa_Bible_1541.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/e8/Jesuites_en_chine.jpg/250px-Jesuites_en_chine.jpg)](/wiki/File:Jesuites_en_chine.jpg)

Top: [Matteo Ricci](/wiki/Matteo_Ricci "Matteo Ricci"), [Adam Schaal](/wiki/Adam_Schall_von_Bell "Adam Schall von Bell") and [Ferdinand Verbiest](/wiki/Ferdinand_Verbiest "Ferdinand Verbiest") (1623–88); Bottom: [Paul Siu (Xu Guangqi)](/wiki/Xu_Guangqi "Xu Guangqi"), *Colao* or Prime Minister of State, and his granddaughter Candide Hiu

For much of its history, the Christian church has been [culturally divided](/wiki/Greek_East_and_Latin_West "Greek East and Latin West") between the Latin-speaking West, whose centre was [Rome](/wiki/Rome "Rome"), and the Greek-speaking East, whose centre was [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"). Cultural differences and political rivalry created tensions between the two churches, leading to disagreement over [doctrine](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_%E2%80%93_Roman_Catholic_theological_differences "Eastern Orthodox – Roman Catholic theological differences") and [ecclesiology](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_%E2%80%93_Roman_Catholic_ecclesiastical_differences "Eastern Orthodox – Roman Catholic ecclesiastical differences") and ultimately to [schism](/wiki/East-West_schism "East-West schism").[[10]](#cite_note-west_christ-10)

Like [Eastern Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Christianity "Eastern Christianity"), Western Christianity traces its roots directly to the [apostles](/wiki/Twelve_Apostles "Twelve Apostles") and other early preachers of the religion. In Western Christianity's original area, [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") was the principal language. Christian writers in Latin had more influence there than those who wrote in [Greek](/wiki/Greek_language "Greek language"), [Syriac](/wiki/Syriac_language "Syriac language"), or other languages. Although the first Christians in the West used Greek (such as [Clement of Rome](/wiki/Clement_of_Rome "Clement of Rome")), by the [fourth century](/wiki/Christianity_in_the_4th_century "Christianity in the 4th century") Latin had superseded it even in the cosmopolitan city of [Rome](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Rome "Early centers of Christianity"), as well as in [southern Gaul](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Southern_Gaul "Early centers of Christianity") and the [Roman province of Africa](/wiki/Early_centers_of_Christianity#Carthage "Early centers of Christianity").[[11]](#cite_note-11) There is evidence of a Latin translation of the [Bible](/wiki/Bible "Bible") as early as the 2nd century (see also [Vetus Latina](/wiki/Vetus_Latina "Vetus Latina")).

With the [decline of the Roman Empire](/wiki/Decline_of_the_Roman_Empire "Decline of the Roman Empire"), distinctions appeared also in organization, since the bishops in the West were not dependent on the [Emperor in Constantinople](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire") and did not come under the influence of the [Caesaropapism in the Eastern Church](/wiki/Caesaropapism#Caesaropapism_in_the_Eastern_Church "Caesaropapism"). While the [see of Constantinople](/wiki/Ecumenical_Patriarch_of_Constantinople "Ecumenical Patriarch of Constantinople") became dominant throughout the Emperor's lands, the West looked exclusively to the [see of Rome](/wiki/Holy_See "Holy See"), which in the East was seen as that of one of the five [patriarchs](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch") of the [Pentarchy](/wiki/Pentarchy_(Christianity) "Pentarchy (Christianity)"), "the proposed government of universal [Christendom](/wiki/Christendom "Christendom") by five [patriarchal](/wiki/Patriarch "Patriarch") sees under the auspices of a single universal empire. Formulated in the legislation of the emperor [Justinian I](/wiki/Justinian_I "Justinian I") (527–565), especially in his [Novella](/wiki/Corpus_Juris_Civilis#Novellae "Corpus Juris Civilis") 131, the theory received formal ecclesiastical sanction at the [Council in Trullo](/wiki/Quinisext_Council "Quinisext Council") (692), which ranked the five sees as Rome, Constantinople, Alexandria, Antioch, and Jerusalem."[[12]](#cite_note-12)

Over the centuries, disagreements separated Western Christianity from the various forms of Eastern Christianity: first from [East Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") after the [Council of Ephesus (431)](/wiki/First_Council_of_Ephesus "First Council of Ephesus"), then from that of [Oriental Orthodoxy](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy "Oriental Orthodoxy") after the [Council of Chalcedon](/wiki/Council_of_Chalcedon "Council of Chalcedon") (451), and then from [Eastern Orthodoxy](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church") with the [East-West Schism](/wiki/East-West_Schism "East-West Schism") of 1054. With the last-named form of Eastern Christianity, reunion agreements were signed at the [Second Council of Lyon](/wiki/Second_Council_of_Lyon "Second Council of Lyon") (1274) and the [Council of Florence](/wiki/Council_of_Florence "Council of Florence") (1439), but these proved ineffective.

Historian Paul Legutko of [Stanford University](/wiki/Stanford_University "Stanford University") said the [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church "Catholic Church") is "at the center of the development of the values, ideas, science, laws, and institutions which constitute what we call [Western civilization](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture")".[[13]](#cite_note-13) The rise of [Protestantism](/wiki/Protestantism "Protestantism") led to major divisions within Western Christianity, which still persist, and wars—for example, the [Anglo-Spanish War of 1585–1604](/wiki/Anglo-Spanish_War_(1585%E2%80%931604) "Anglo-Spanish War (1585–1604)") had religious as well as economic causes.

In and after the [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery"), [Europeans](/wiki/Europe "Europe") spread Western Christianity to the [New World](/wiki/New_World "New World") and elsewhere. Roman Catholicism came to the Americas (especially South America), Africa, Asia, Australia and the Pacific. Protestantism, including Anglicanism, came to North America, Australia-Pacific and some African locales.

Today, the geographical distinction between Western and Eastern Christianity is much less absolute, due to the great migrations of Europeans across the globe, as well as the work of [missionaries](/wiki/Missionary "Missionary") worldwide over the past five centuries.

### Original sin

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Original sin")]

[Original sin](/wiki/Original_sin "Original sin"), also called [ancestral sin](/wiki/Ancestral_sin "Ancestral sin"),[[14]](#cite_note-Golitzin1995-14)[[15]](#cite_note-Tate2005-15)[[16]](#cite_note-Bartolo-Abela2011-16)[[17]](#cite_note-Hassan2012-17) is a [Christian](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity") belief in a [state of sin](/wiki/State_of_sin "State of sin") in which humanity has existed since the [fall of man](/wiki/Fall_of_man "Fall of man"), stemming from [Adam and Eve](/wiki/Adam_and_Eve "Adam and Eve")'s rebellion in the [Garden of Eden](/wiki/Garden_of_Eden "Garden of Eden"), namely the sin of disobedience in consuming the [forbidden fruit](/wiki/Forbidden_fruit "Forbidden fruit") from the [tree of the knowledge of good and evil](/wiki/Tree_of_the_knowledge_of_good_and_evil "Tree of the knowledge of good and evil").[[18]](#cite_note-18) Theologians have characterized this condition in many ways, seeing it as ranging from something as insignificant as a slight deficiency, or a tendency toward sin yet without [collective guilt](/wiki/Collective_accountability "Collective accountability"), referred to as a "sin nature", to something as drastic as [total depravity](/wiki/Total_depravity "Total depravity") or automatic guilt of all humans through collective guilt.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

### Filioque clause

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Filioque clause")]

See also: [Filioque](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque")

Most Western Christians use a version of the [Nicene Creed](/wiki/Nicene_Creed "Nicene Creed") that states that the [Holy Spirit](/wiki/Holy_Spirit_in_Christianity "Holy Spirit in Christianity") "[proceeds from the Father and the Son](/wiki/Filioque "Filioque")", where the original text as adopted by the [First Council of Constantinople](/wiki/First_Council_of_Constantinople "First Council of Constantinople") had "proceeds from the Father" without the addition of either "and the Son" or "alone". This Western version also has the additional phrase "God from God" ([Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language"): *Deum de Deo*), which was in the Creed as adopted by the [First Council of Nicaea](/wiki/First_Council_of_Nicaea "First Council of Nicaea"), but which was dropped by the First Council of Constantinople.

### Date of Easter

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Date of Easter")]

Main article: [Easter controversy](/wiki/Easter_controversy "Easter controversy")

The [date of Easter](/wiki/Easter#Date "Easter") usually differs between Eastern and Western Christianity, because the calculations are based on the [Julian calendar](/wiki/Julian_calendar "Julian calendar") and [Gregorian calendar](/wiki/Gregorian_calendar "Gregorian calendar") respectively.[[20]](#cite_note-20) However, before the Council of Nicea, various dates including Jewish Passover were observed. Nicea "Romanized" the date for Easter and anathematized a "Judaized" (i.e. Passover date for) Easter. The date of observance of Easter has only differed in modern times since the promulgation of the Gregorian calendar in 1582; and further, the Western Church did not universally adopt the Gregorian calendar at once, so that for some time the dates of Easter differed between the Eastern Church and the Roman Catholic Church, but not necessarily as between the Eastern Church and the Western Protestant churches. For example, the Church of England continued to observe Easter on the same date as the Eastern Church until 1753.

Even the dates of other Christian holidays often differ between Eastern and Western Christianity.[[21]](#cite_note-21)

### Lack of essence–energies distinction

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Lack of essence–energies distinction")]

Further information: [Essence–energies distinction](/wiki/Essence%E2%80%93energies_distinction "Essence–energies distinction")

Eastern Christianity, and particularly the Eastern Orthodox Church, has traditionally held a distinction between God's essence, or that which He is, with God's energies, or that which He does. They hold that while God is unknowable in His essence, He can be known (i.e. experienced) in His energies. This is an extension of Eastern Christianity's [apophatic theology](/wiki/Apophatic_theology "Apophatic theology"), while Western Christians tend to prefer a view of [divine simplicity](/wiki/Divine_simplicity "Divine simplicity"), and claim that God's essence can be known by its attributes.

## Western denominations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Western denominations")]

See also: [Western Rite Orthodoxy](/wiki/Western_Rite_Orthodoxy "Western Rite Orthodoxy")

Today, Western Christianity makes up close to 90% of [Christians worldwide](/wiki/Christianity_by_country "Christianity by country") with the Catholic Church accounting for over half and various Protestant denominations making up another 40%.

[Hussite](/wiki/Hussites "Hussites") movements of 15th century [Bohemia](/wiki/Bohemia "Bohemia") preceded the main Protestant uprising by 100 years and evolved into several small Protestant churches, such as the [Moravian Church](/wiki/Moravian_Church "Moravian Church"). [Waldensians](/wiki/Waldensians "Waldensians") survived also, but blended into the [Reformed tradition](/wiki/Reformed_tradition "Reformed tradition").

### The Reformers

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Western_Christianity&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Reformers")]

Further information: [Protestant Reformers](/wiki/Protestant_Reformers "Protestant Reformers")

Relevant figures:

* [Jan Hus](/wiki/Jan_Hus "Jan Hus") (c. 1369–1415), one of the most relevant theologians in the 14th century.
* [Martin Luther](/wiki/Martin_Luther "Martin Luther") (1483–1546), the most famous reformer and theologian in the Reformation and in the 15th century.
* [Jan Łaski](/wiki/Jan_%C5%81aski "Jan Łaski") (1499–1560), known also as John à Lasco, leading Polish Calvinist reformer and a secretary of Polish King [Sigismund II](/wiki/Sigismund_II_Augustus "Sigismund II Augustus")
* [Hans Tausen](/wiki/Hans_Tausen "Hans Tausen"), Bishop of [Ribe](/wiki/Ribe "Ribe") (1494–1561), leading theologian of the [Reformation in Denmark and Holstein](/wiki/Reformation_in_Denmark%E2%80%93Norway_and_Holstein "Reformation in Denmark–Norway and Holstein").
* [Laurentius Petri](/wiki/Laurentius_Petri "Laurentius Petri"), [Archbishop of Uppsala and all Sweden](/wiki/List_of_archbishops_of_Uppsala "List of archbishops of Uppsala") (1499–1573), along with his brother [Olaus Petri](/wiki/Olaus_Petri "Olaus Petri") were regarded as the main Lutheran reformers of Sweden, together with the king [Gustav I of Sweden](/wiki/Gustav_I_of_Sweden "Gustav I of Sweden").
* [Primož Trubar](/wiki/Primo%C5%BE_Trubar "Primož Trubar") (1508–1586), mostly known as the author of the first [Slovene language](/wiki/Slovene_language "Slovene language") [printed](/wiki/Printing_press "Printing press") book,[[22]](#cite_note-SBL-22) the founder and the first superintendent of the Protestant Church of the [Duchy of Carniola](/wiki/Duchy_of_Carniola "Duchy of Carniola"), and for consolidating the [Slovenian language](/wiki/Slovenian_language "Slovenian language").
* [John Calvin](/wiki/John_Calvin "John Calvin") (1509–1564)
* [John Knox](/wiki/John_Knox "John Knox") (1514–1572)
* [Ferenc Dávid](/wiki/Ferenc_D%C3%A1vid "Ferenc Dávid") (1520–1579) founder of the [Unitarian Church of Transylvania](/wiki/Unitarian_Church_of_Transylvania "Unitarian Church of Transylvania") who laid the foundation for what would become the [nontrinitarian](/wiki/Nontrinitarianism "Nontrinitarianism") movement.
* [Mikael Agricola](/wiki/Mikael_Agricola "Mikael Agricola"), [bishop of Turku](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Turku "Archdiocese of Turku") (1554–1557), he became the de facto founder of literary Finnish and a prominent proponent of the Protestant Reformation in Sweden, including Finland, which was a Swedish territory at the time. He is often called the "father of literary Finnish".
